7.1 Do it Yourself!  
7.2 My Solution  
7.2.1 The Player  
7.2.2 The Enemy  
Creating the Enemy Actor  
Our enemy, similar to the Player Projectile, will consist of a new actor with a new script as well as a  
model.  
Create a new actor entitled Enemy Actor.  
Create a new script for it and save it as Enemy.ai.  
Assign a model to him - let's use Elf_Near.mdl in C:\tfb\content\models\characters\.  
Give him a collision volume - a Capsule 3 feet wide and 7 feet high.  
So our enemy is not so fast that he can catch the player without any eﬀort, set his ground max  
speed to 8 feet per second.  
At this point you may build and launch to see your enemy standing there, in all his glory. He's not  
doing much, yet, but you can verify he shows up.  
Attaching a Weapon  
This enemy model is designed to have a weapon attached to it - a big spear, to be speciﬁc. We'll make  
a new actor which uses the spear model and then use a script command to create and attach the spear.  
2 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Create a new actor entitled Enemy Weapon.  
Assign the spear model ElfNearSpear.mdl (say that 10 times fast!) which lives in C:\tfb\content  
\models\characters\.  
You should see the spear in the 3D view. Mark the actor as a template.  
On completion, the spear actor should look like this:  
Now that we have a spear template set up, we can set up the enemy actor's script to clone and attach  
a copy when it starts up. Attaching is done by setting the actor's *attach node reference.  
Open the Enemy Actor's script for edit, and, in the STARTUP section, add a new clone line.  
Set it to clone the Enemy Weapon, under (ACTORS) > Enemy Weapon > <self>.  
Add a new set *reference line within the clone block.  
Set the lefthand parameter to ^cloned's > *attach node's > <self>.  
Set the righthand parameter to my > <self>.  
When you're all done, the startup should look like this:  
Since the creation of a new actor won't live update, you should build and launch now. You'll notice that  
the enemy does, indeed, have a spear, but it's attached to his origin - right at his feet. What we really  
want is to attach it to his hands, as his animations are set up to swing it around and attack with it.  
Open up the Enemy Actor's model in the Model Editor and switch to the tab entitled Special  
3 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Nodes.  
These are the model's attach modes - you'll notice that several have been created, already. Try  
selecting the various nodes, and you'll see that a marker appears in the 3d view to denote where the  
attach node is. You can even add a preview model to the attach node by clicking the Attachment  
proxy mesh Maya ﬁle name button towards the bottom.  
Click the proxy mesh button and select ElfNearSpear.ma in C:\tfb\content\Models\characters  
\MinionsMonsters\ElfNear\Rig (the ﬁle select dialog should take you there).  
Now you can see the proxy mesh as it would appear attached to the various nodes. By highlighting  
node 0, I can tell that this is the right attach node since the spear looks like it's in the right place. You  
can even preview animations while examing attach nodes.  
Click back to the Mesh and Animation tab, select an animation, and press the Play button at the  
bottom. You'll see the enemy animate with spear in hand.  
Now that you've found the right attach node (node 0) for the spear, let's leave the Model Editor and  
adjust our enemy's script.  
Adjust the righthand side of the set *reference line to my > [attach nodes] > <self>.  
4 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Just like when we were playing animations in the player's script, now that you're pointing at a [set],  
the oﬀset by parameter appears.  
We wanted node 0 anyway, so we can leave the oﬀset as it is.  
Now, save the script to live update it. Since the STARTUP of a script happens only when the actor  
starts up, live updating the script, alone, wouldn't be suﬀicient to witness the change we made in the  
enemy script. We need to force the actor to shut down and start back up. A neat trick we can use is to  
check the template box on him, which will turn him into a template, shutting him down, and then  
uncheck it, turning him back into a live, placed actor.  
Check and uncheck the Template box on the Enemy Actor now.  
You should see the enemy disappear when you check the box and reappear when you uncheck it, with  
spear in hand. However, notice the old spear still lying on the ground. The problem is that our enemy is  
cloning an actor whenever it starts up but not removing it when it shuts down.  
Cleaning up On Shutdown  
We have just demonstrated one of the two most commonly created memory leaks in our system -  
something is being created but never destroyed. Managing this problem is really easy, luckily. We'll  
create an actor *reference to hang on to the spear and then, in the SHUTDOWN, we'll remove it.  
Right-click to add a new create variable command to make a new actor *reference at the top of  
the enemy script's startup.  
5 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Name it something like spear actor.  
Add a new set *reference command to the clone block.  
Set the lefthand parameter to (LOCALS) > *spear actor (it may have automagically been set  
already).  
Set the righthand parameter to ^cloned's > <self>.  
Now the *spear actor *reference is pointing to the cloned spear. Now we can add the remove  
command to the SHUTDOWN.  
In the SHUTDOWN block, add a new remove command and set the parameter to be the spear  
reference, in (LOCALS) > *spear actor > <self>.  
Superb! Now, whenever the enemy starts up, he will keep a reference to the spear he cloned, and  
whenever he shuts down, he'll remove it. What a nice, tidy enemy.  
Just to reiterate my point about this being one of our common memory leaks, just remember this  
golden rule: If a script is creating something, someone better be removing that something, later.  
Enemy AI  
Okay, we've got an enemy, he's got a spear, but he really isn't doing anything very interesting or  
enemy-like yet. We'll create a very simple A.I. for the enemy where he stands still, runs at the player if  
the player gets near, attacks the player when he's in melee range, and then returns to his initial  
location. Finally, we can add some logic for not chasing the player forever, in case the player gets too  
far away.  
NOTE: As we go through the scripting steps, feel free to live update as much as you like to see  
what the enemy is doing!  
Structuring our Behaviors  
6 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Since we've already covered the basics of how behaviors work, I'm not going to detail all those steps  
again. When I'm building something like an enemy A.I., I think about it like an essay: start with the  
outline (the behaviors and what they're going to do), then ﬁll in the content later. Feel free to develop  
your own approach that works for you, but while we're using my methodology, we'll create the  
following behaviors:  
An idle behavior, the initial behavior, where the enemy will just be standing there.  
A chase behavior, where the enemy will chase after the player.  
An attack behavior, where the enemy will strike the player.  
A run home behavior, where the enemy will return to his initial position.  
Now that we have an idea of what sort of structure we want, let's create the behaviors.  
Create each of the behaviors listed above in the enemy's script.  
Thinking About our Behaviors  
Let's take a brief design detour here - this has nothing to do with actual implementation, just the  
thought process behind it. We know what, in general, we want the enemy to do, but now we can  
specify even further on a per-behavior basis. The two important components in each behavior, just like  
the player's script, are what happens while we're here and on what condition does the behavior  
change (and to what). Talking about the design side before we script it:  
idle  
What Happens: play the idle animation  
Behavior Changes  
When the player is near, chase  
chase  
What Happens: run after the player  
Behavior Changes  
When I get close enough to the player, attack  
When I get too far from my starting point, run home  
When the player gets too far from me, run home  
attack  
What Happens: play an attack animation to attack the player  
Behavior Changes  
When the animation is done, chase  
run home  
What Happens: run back to my starting point  
Behavior Changes  
When I reach my starting point, idle  
Just as a note, you absolutely don't need to design out the whole thing before you start implementing.  
One of the strengths of our tool is that you can change whatever you like on the ﬂy. I'm just listing out  
my though process as I build the enemy, so you have an idea of where I'm coming from.  
7 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Checking for the Player  
Before starting, I'd recommend opening the enemy's model in the Model Editor, since we'll be looking  
up various animations to play from there.  
NOTE: At this point, since we're using commands we've used before, I'm going to assume you're  
comfortable with the selection of parameters from our context menus. I will detail where to ﬁnd  
the parameters in the text but won't be providing snapshots of what the menu looks like.  
Let's start from the top behavior and work our way down.  
The idle will be very straightforward: we want to play the idle animation and check if the player is  
close enough to the threshold when we want to start chasing him.  
Start by adding a new play command to the beginning of the idle behavior, setting the parameter  
to (ANIMATIONS) > [animation palette] and specifying slot 0 as the oﬀset (the idle  
animation's slot).  
Add a new check value command, and check if (ACTORS) > Player Actor's > distance from  
myself is less than (<) 20 feet.  
Finally, within the check value block, add a new set behavior command and set it to the chase  
behavior.  
Done! When the player comes closer than 20 feet away, the enemy will switch to the chase behavior.  
Moving To the Player  
Our player is propelled by being assigned a *controller and the act of your moving the controller's  
stick around. In the case of the enemy, we need a way to tell him to move around in script. One way to  
accomplish this is with the move to command, which lets you specify a location and then moves the  
actor there using its movement parameters.  
Add a new move to command to the beginning of the chase behavior.  
8 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Set the ﬁrst parameter to (ACTORS) > Player Actor > <self>.  
If you were to run right now, once the enemy switched to the chase behavior, he would immediately  
move towards the player. Note that the move to is a block of script, like a check value - it will be true  
when the until within component is satisﬁed. The enemy will never be within exactly 0 feet of the  
player, though, so let's set a more tolerant number.  
Set the until within to 8 feet, so he has a condition that says he's close enough.  
Finally, let's have him play his run animation, which, by looking at the Model Editor, we can see  
lives in slot 1 of his [animation palette].  
Now, add a new set behavior command to the move to block, so when the enemy comes within  
range of the player, he changes to the attack behavior.  
File:04e moveto.png  
Copying and Pasting  
Now for the rest of the chase behavior, starting with checking if we're too far from the player. While we  
could add a new check value command, we already have a line that looks really similar in the above  
idle behavior. I like to be as lazy, erm... eﬀicient as possible, so why not copy and paste that line?  
Copying and pasting works a lot like moving lines as far as selection and placement are concerned.  
Select the check value line in the idle behavior.  
From the upper menu, select Edit > Copy (or hit Ctrl+C).  
Highlight ﬂow end in the chase behavior - for built-in, immovable lines like a ﬂow end, you will  
not see the big highlight bar, just an underscoring of the text.  
9 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
From the upper menu, select Edit > Paste (or hit Ctrl+V).  
Voila! You have copied and pasted the whole block of script, and now you can tweak it to ﬁt your  
desired settings.  
Finishing the Chase Behavior  
Adjust the check value line to check if the player is beyond 30 feet, and, if so, go to the run  
home behavior.  
We also want to run home if we get too far from our starting point.  
Paste the same block again (you don't need to copy again - it's still in the buﬀer for pasting), but  
this time, check if my > origin > distance is greater than 50 feet away - if so, change to the  
run home behavior.  
The origin is a point in space which is where an actor was located when it started up. In the case of  
the enemy, it is wherever you place him in the world. In the case of a cloned object, it is wherever he  
was cloned.  
Animation Restarting  
10 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Now let's specify the attack behavior, which is much more straightforward. We want to play an  
animation, then, when the animation is done, return to the chase behavior. We have an implementation  
decision to make, though: if we're going to track whether or not the attack animation is done, we  
need some way of playing it, remembering that we already played it, then checking if it's done without  
confusing that with it not yet being played at all.  
In the attack behavior, if you just placed a play command, and below, checked its time remaining, the  
animation would restart as soon as it ﬁnished because it's hitting the play command repeatedly. Once  
the animation has 0 time remaining, the play would execute again. You'd get stuck in the behavior.  
When playing animations, the play command will not actually execute if the animation it's being told to  
play is already playing. If you added a print to host inside the play block, it would only print once,  
then wait for the animation to ﬁnish, then print once again, etc. Though it's safe to spam the play  
command, keep in mind that you may get weird results like in the above example.  
NOTE: We could solve the issue by reversing the play and check value commands in the above  
example, but I would call this a lousy solution - it suﬀers from being very, very indirect, and it is  
prone to a lot of breakage, especially if we want to do anything more advanced. Let's learn good  
habits instead.  
Though there are several solutions to the issue (which we will revisit later), I think we should start the  
animation when transitioning out of the chase behavior, since we can very easily ensure it only runs  
once. At this point, the attack behavior becomes more of a check when attack is done behavior. We  
could even rename it as such, if we wanted to.  
The Attack Animation  
Perusing the elf model in the Model Editor, I think the AttackHit animation in slot 22 is a good  
candidate for our attack animation. Feel free to check it out now.  
Since we'll need to track the completion of the attack animation, we need a new animation  
*reference. Create one in the startup and name it appropriately.  
Add a new play command inside the block of the check value where we're changing to the  
attack behavior and set it to play from slot 22 of the [animation palette].  
Add a new set *reference command inside the play block to hang on to the animation.  
11 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
In the attack behavior, add a new check value command and check if the *attack animation's  
time remaining is equal to 0.  
Add a new set behavior command inside the check value's block to go back to the chase  
behavior when the animation completes.  
Returning to the Origin  
Now we just need to specify how the enemy should run home - we'll have him return to his origin  
with a move to command.  
Add a new move to command to the run home behavior, and set the enemy to move to his origin  
until within 3 feet. Have him play his run animation from slot 1 as well.  
Add a new set behavior command inside the move to's block to return to the idle behavior once  
the enemy gets to his origin.  
Printing the Behavior  
If you haven't been running or live updating the game, be sure to do so now and check out the enemy!  
He should behave as expected, and you can verify he's doing what you think he is with the print  
command.  
In the enemy's PRESCRIPT, add a print to target that prints the behavior.  
You should see some text on-screen now that speciﬁes the current behavior.  
Tweaking the Camera  
12 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
With our enemy chasing the player around, the camera feels a little close to see the action. Let's adjust  
the Camera Actor's script by setting it to displace further out than the current 40 feet.  
Open the Camera script and change the displace's length to be something like 70 feet.  
Alright! Now that we can see the action and have a good sense of what the enemy is doing, let's create  
some interaction between the player and the enemy scripts.  
Damaging the Enemy/Player  
Having a player and enemy that can attack each other isn't very interesting if the attacks don't actually  
cause any eﬀects. Let's set up the player to be able to hurt the enemy, and vice-versa.  
Detecting Enemy Collision  
Let's start by having the player's projectile attack be able to hit the enemy actor. For that, we'll be  
checking collision between each of the actors, which checks if the collision volumes of each of the  
objects are intersecting each other. We can access that information with our old friend, check value.  
Open the Player Projectile's script and add a new check value line above the separation check.  
Adjust the lefthand parameter to check my > collision.  
Set it to check it for collision with (ACTORS) > Enemy Actor > <self>.  
13 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Finally, set the righthand side to check if the collision is true.  
The ﬁnal line should read as follows.  
Now that we have a method for detecting the collision, we need a way to tell the enemy that he has  
been hit.  
Communicating Between Scripts: Design  
There are several ways to communicate information between scripts, but the issue is always about  
exposing whatever needs to be communicated. For example, distance between two actors is something  
that's naturally exposed - you can write a check value in any script to check a distance.  
In this case, we've started to add some logic to the projectile, which will look for collision with the  
enemy. Once the collision happens, we need some way of telling the enemy that he has been hit so the  
enemy can react appropriately. The enemy could also have a check that looks a lot like the one we  
added to the projectile, but I consider that a poor design for a few reasons, which I will circle back  
around to cover after we've ﬁnished our projectile's hurt implementation.  
The simplest solution, in this case, will be to create a value variable in the enemy's script, which the  
enemy will check to see if he has been hurt, then have the projectile set it upon collision.  
Creating a Value Variable  
We've created *reference variables to hang on to things like animations or actors, thus far, but we can  
also create a plain ol' value variable, which is just a number. Like distance, facing, and other built-in  
values we've used so far, we can set and check it, giving it as much meaning and purpose as we want.  
In this case, we'll make a hurt value to detect if the enemy has been damaged by something - like our  
projectile - and then play a reaction.  
Open the enemy's script and add a new value variable to the STARTUP with create variable.  
Name it hurt.  
14 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Now, in the enemy's PRESCRIPT, add a check value to see if hurt is equal to 1. We add it to  
the prescript because we want the enemy to always be checking to see if he's been hurt.  
Create a new behavior for the enemy and name it hurt behavior, then, in our above check  
value, add a new set behavior command to set the behavior to hurt behavior.  
The Hurt Animation  
Alright, we're partway there. We can check if the hurt value is 1, then change behaviors appropriately.  
We still need to handle the reaction, which we'll do with an animation, and then manage returning to  
the normal behavior cycle. We'll take a similar approach as we did with the attack animation - creating  
an animation *reference, playing the animation, hanging on to the animation, then changing  
behaviors when the animation completes.  
Create a new animation *reference in the STARTUP and name it hurt animation.  
When we change behaviors to the hurt behavior, let's play a hurt animation. Looking in the Model  
Editor, the animation in slot 41 looks good.  
Add a new play command to the hurt section to play animation 41, then, set the *hurt animation  
reference to hang on to the ^playing animation.  
Down in the hurt behavior, add a new check value command to check if the animation has  
completed, then, if so, go to the chase behavior.  
15 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
NOTE: Why the chase behavior? It makes the most sense to me - if the player just shot the enemy, it's  
likely the player is near, or, at the very least, the enemy should react by trying to chase the player  
down. If the player happened to shoot the enemy from 100 feet away for some reason, the enemy  
would go to chase, but then the line checking if the player is too far would return true, and he would,  
next frame, wind up in run home.  
Find Variable  
Now that we have some hurt logic set up in the enemy, which responds to the hurt value, we need a  
way to set that value in the enemy's script when the projectile collides with the enemy. When we deal  
with created variables, they appear in the (LOCALS) section when we set/check them, as opposed to  
things like distance which are part of (ACTORS) (or myself, analogous to picking the actor from the  
actors menu). We now want to set the hurt value, which lives in the enemy's script, from the projectile.  
Add a new set value command to the projectile's script when it detects collision, and pull open  
the parameter menu on the lefthand side.  
Looking at the possible options, note the absence of (LOCALS). It makes sense, as our projectile script  
has no local variables. The variable we want to access is in the enemy's script, so how do we get at it?  
The answer is the ﬁnd variable command, which lets you access another actors' local variables.  
Remove the set value you added, which was just for demonstration purposes.  
Add a new ﬁnd variable command where the set value was, and select a (VALUES) > <self>  
type.  
Note that the submenu looks a lot like the create variable command. That is because you need to  
specify what type of variable you're looking for so the scripting language knows what sorts of  
16 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
parameters it should have.  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Type in the name of the variable you're looking for. In this case, hurt.  
Hit enter to close the text entry box. Now that you've speciﬁed what the type and name of the  
variable is, you must specify the actor who runs the script that owns it. Select the Enemy Actor  
as the parameter for the owned by.  
Now that we have access to the hurt variable in the enemy's script, add a new set value  
command within the ﬁnd variable's block to set ^hurt to 1. Note that ^hurt showed up with a  
^, to indicate that it is a scoped variable, and we'll only have access to it while inside the ﬁnd  
variable block, just like ^playing for the play command.  
Superb! Now, when the projectile collides with the enemy, it will set the enemy's hurt value to 1. Build  
and launch (or live update, but be sure all your scripts have been updated!) and let's check out what  
happens when we shoot the enemy.  
Cleaning Up  
After you shoot the enemy, you'll notice he seems to loop his hurt animation over and over. There's a  
logical issue with our enemy script. We check if hurt is 1, and, in that case, we play the hurt animation  
and change behaviors to the hurt behavior. In that behavior, we check to see if the animation is done to  
decide when to change behaviors. That said hurt will stay 1 forever, forcing the enemy to constantly  
set his behavior to hurt behavior and play the animation over and over.  
17 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
We need to apply a technique called debouncing for the hurt value by setting it back to 0 after we  
respond to it. That way, we won't respond to it repeatedly.  
Add a new set value command within the hurt check to set the hurt value back to 0. I like to put  
this sort of thing in the very top of the check to indicate very clearly that it is a debounce, since  
the set value relates directly to the above check value.  
Save the script to live update, and you'll notice the enemy frees himself from the hurt behavior once  
the animation is done. Perfect! Now try shooting him again - you'll see that the enemy seems to  
repeatedly play the hurt animation while the projectile ﬂies through him. The problem is that the  
projectile is constantly colliding with the enemy, so our check for collision and subsequent ﬁnd  
variable is setting the enemy's hurt value to 1, stepping on any debouncing we might be doing. I'd  
like the projectile to only hurt the enemy once, but, more importantly, I think it should remove itself  
when it hits the enemy.  
Add a new remove line to the projectile to have it remove itself when it collides with the enemy.  
Live update the projectile script, and now try shooting the enemy. The projectile should vanish when it  
collides with the enemy, and the enemy will only play his hurt reaction animation once.  
Why Implement Like This?  
Recall, earlier, that I mentioned that this was the nicest way to implement the solution to this problem.  
18 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
Now that you've gone through the steps, I think it's worth covering the reasons why we did it this way,  
as opposed to having both enemy and player check for collision with one another.  
Avoiding duplicate work: The enemy and the projectile would eﬀectively be copying the same line  
of script. Creating, much less maintaining, something in two places is harder than one.  
Localizing control: It is a good scripting practice to remember, as you go forward, that, whenever  
possible, the ﬂow of control should be as localized as possible. This helps with the aforementioned  
maintenance, but it also helps you keep your scripts sane and predictable. The projectile only  
knows to set a variable, and it's up to the enemy to respond however it sees ﬁt.  
Race conditions: It would be unfortunate if things happened in an unexpected order. Will the  
enemy detect collision ﬁrst, or will the projectile? We can't predict that, so we could get  
unusual results. Imagine if the projectile detected collision ﬁrst, then it removed itself right  
away. If the enemy looked for collision with the projectile to detect being hurt, the projectile  
would be gone by the time it got around to it!  
There are more reasons why we structured it this way, but they will become clear as we continue to  
develop and improve our game. I'll circle around, yet again, later to talk more about the implications of  
our good design.  
Hurting the Player  
Now that the player can hurt the enemy, it's only fair to let the enemy hurt him back.  
Do it Yourself!  
It's time to let you oﬀ the leash! We just implemented a technique for hurting the enemy, but now I'd  
like you to think about how to use the same concept to have the enemy's attack hurt the player. I'm not  
going to walk through all the steps, since you know most of them, and I want you to think about the  
ones you don't necessarily know oﬀ-hand. Very similar to the projectile/enemy interaction, the general  
procedure you want is:  
Player has a hurt variable.  
Enemy sets the hurt variable.  
QUESTION: How can you make sure the enemy doesn't repeatedly set the hurt variable in  
the player?  
QUESTION: How can you properly detect that the enemy's spear is hitting the player?  
Player checks the hurt variable, debounces it, plays an animation, and switches behaviors when  
hurt.  
HINT: Check in the Model Editor to ﬁnd a good animation for the player. Try to use a base  
type animation, not a partial (the radio button in the editor and the fact that the animation  
will look strange when you play it will indicate it being partial).  
Player returns to his normal behavior after the hurt animation completes.  
At this point, you should STOP READING and try to implement it on your own. Keep going when  
you're ready to check out my solution or if you get stuck.  
Did you stop?  
Are you done?  
Ready for my solution?  
Careful, once you see it, you can't un-see it...  
My Solution  
19 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
If you're here, I'll assume you gave it a stab and got it working or are having trouble. Either is ﬁne! If  
you're just lazy and skipped over the do it yourself part, though, shame on you. Your insolence and IP  
have been logged on our server. Anyway, below I'll explain the steps I took and include pictures of the  
pertinent scripts.  
The Player  
The player component was very straightforward. I almost mirrored what we did with the enemy, but  
with one notable exception. Instead of naming my hurt variable the same as the enemy's, I named it  
player hurt. Why? Idiot-prooﬁng. Look at the two ﬁnd variable blocks below:  
Can you spot the diﬀerence? Look at the set value line. The lefthand one sets ^hurt (with a carat) and  
the righthand one sets hurt (without a carat). It would be very easy to screw up and accidentally  
create a line that sets a local variable instead of the found variable. By naming the player's hurt  
variable distinctly, my script is more legible and harder to screw up.  
For my version of the player, I used the eat food animation as his hurt, in slot 7.  
Below is the ﬁnished version of the player's script (red arrows denote newly-added sections).  
The Enemy  
The enemy was a bit trickier. I had the following problems to solve:  
How do I detect that the spear is actually hitting the player? I don't want to hurt the player if the  
player happens to dodge the spear.  
Even if I detect that, how can I make sure the hurting only happens at certain moments in the  
animation? The enemy's attack has very clear times when the spear seems dangerous, and some  
other parts of the animation are just a wind-up or recovery.  
Finally, unlike the projectile, I will not be able to remove the spear to stop it from hurting the  
player repeatedly. How do I make sure I only hurt the player once per swing?  
20 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
Solving each problem in order:  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
I gave the spear a collision volume to allow me to make collision checks from the spear actor as  
the enemy swung it, making sure to uncheck the physical interaction checkboxes, since I don't  
want it actually, physically colliding with anything.  
Then, in the enemy's script, I had him check the *spear actor's collision with the player.  
To deal with the issue of the animation having moments where I didn't want to hurt the player, I  
checked if the animation's percent remaining was within a certain range. You could do it with  
time remaiming, instead, but it's easier for me to conceptualize percent since it's always a ﬁxed  
range (100% to 0%).  
Finally, I needed a way of detecting whether or not I had already hurt the player, so I didn't spam  
the hurt line. I created a local already did hurt value variable to track whether or not I had  
already hurt the player. I set it to 0 every time I start the attack, and, once I hurt the player, I set  
it to 1 to prevent further hurting until a new attack is started.  
21 of 22  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.2:_Intermediate...  
These were just my solutions! You probably came up with some of your own solutions, and they may  
work for you as well as mine. Hopefully my thought process will help guide you as you solve things for  
yourself. There are as many ways to solve these problems as there are ways to skin a cat. Our tool  
doesn't skin cats, though.  
Our tool, however, provides many other features which we can use to further improve and enhance our  
little game. Continue on to the third part of Intermediate Scripting to learn more.
